title: IELTS Reading — Cambridge 11 Test 3
bandHint: IELTS Academic · Cambridge 11 · Test 3 · 3 passages · 40 câu

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 1–13.

passage.label: A
passage.text: City planners once treated parks as decorative extras. Today many municipalities rank green space as essential infrastructure that improves air quality and reduces flooding.

passage.label: B
passage.text: Researchers comparing neighbourhoods found that residents living within a ten-minute walk of a park reported lower stress levels, though the study could not prove causation.

passage.label: C
passage.text: Some councils resist large park projects because maintenance budgets are already strained. Critics argue that planting trees along streets delivers similar benefits at lower cost.

passage.label: D
passage.text: Volunteer groups have restored abandoned lots into community gardens. These projects depend on local fundraising and rarely receive national grants.

passage.label: E
passage.text: Architects increasingly design buildings with rooftop gardens. Supporters claim these spaces should be mandatory on new commercial developments.

passage.label: F
passage.text: Historical records show that Victorian cities invested heavily in public parks to improve public health. Modern campaigns often cite this precedent.

passage.label: G
passage.text: International surveys suggest that cities with generous green coverage attract skilled workers, but the relationship between greenery and economic growth remains debated.

questionGroups.instruction: Reading Passage 1 has seven paragraphs, A–G. Choose the correct heading for each paragraph from the list of headings below.
questionGroups.note: There are more headings than paragraphs, so you will not use all of them.

headings.label: Financial obstacles to large-scale park projects
A shift in how cities value open space
Evidence linking proximity to parks with wellbeing
Grassroots efforts to reclaim unused land
Debate over whether rooftop gardens should be compulsory
Lessons drawn from nineteenth-century urban planning
Uncertainty about economic effects of urban greenery
Why street trees are cheaper than formal parks

questions.explanation: Paragraph A describes a shift from decorative parks to essential infrastructure.

questions.prompt: Paragraph G

questions.explanation: The government launched its first attempt to save the kākāpō in 1894.

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 14–26.
parts.passageSubtitle: Mark Rowe investigates attempts to reintroduce elms to Britain

passage.label: A
passage.text: Around 25 million elms died in Britain in the 1960s and 1970s from Dutch elm disease. The loss of 90% of Britain's elms changed the landscape. Today, conservationists hope to reintroduce elms in central and southern England. Matt Elliot of the Woodland Trust says disease-resistant strains offer real hope.

passage.label: B
passage.text: Dutch elm disease is caused by a fungus that blocks the tree's vascular system. The first epidemic arrived in the 1920s. A far more destructive epidemic followed in the 1970s after infected elm logs were imported from Canada. Bark beetles spread the fungus between trees.

passage.label: C
passage.text: Elms can still be found in southern England, though mainly as small hedgerow trees. When the trunk reaches 10–15 cm in diameter, the tree becomes vulnerable to beetles carrying the disease. Larger surviving elms are now extremely rare.

passage.label: D
passage.text: One valley in Scotland has remained largely free of the disease because of its isolation and climate. Researchers study these locations to understand how some areas escape infection while neighbouring regions do not.

passage.label: E
passage.text: Some landowners disagree about whether reintroducing elms is worthwhile. Critics argue that resources should focus on native species already under threat. Supporters believe elm avenues are part of Britain's cultural heritage and worth restoring.

passage.label: F
passage.text: Scientists working with surviving mature elms face practical difficulties because so few large specimens remain. Collecting genetic material from scattered trees slows breeding programmes designed to produce resistant varieties.

passage.label: G
passage.text: New planting schemes combine resistant cultivars with careful monitoring. Young trees are inspected regularly so that infection can be detected before it spreads. The aim is not only to restore elm numbers but to learn how to manage future outbreaks.

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 27–40.
parts.passageTitle: The science of sleep and memory

passage.text: Sleep has long been linked to memory, but only in recent decades have researchers begun to understand the mechanisms involved. Studies show that different stages of sleep support different kinds of learning, from factual recall to motor skills.

During slow-wave sleep, the brain appears to replay experiences from the day, strengthening neural connections that matter and weakening those that do not. This process may explain why students who sleep after revision often perform better in tests.

REM sleep, by contrast, is associated with more creative forms of problem solving. Volunteers woken during REM sleep sometimes solve puzzles more quickly after returning to sleep, suggesting that unusual associations are formed during this stage.

Not all findings are conclusive. Some experiments use small sample sizes, and laboratory sleep differs from sleep at home. Even so, schools and workplaces are increasingly aware that cutting sleep reduces productivity and long-term retention.

questionGroups.instruction: Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 3? Write TRUE, FALSE or NOT GIVEN.

questions.prompt: Scientists have fully explained how sleep affects memory.
questions.explanation: The passage says researchers have only recently begun to understand mechanisms.

questions.prompt: Slow-wave sleep may strengthen useful neural connections.
questions.explanation: Slow-wave sleep strengthens important connections and weakens others.

questions.prompt: REM sleep is mainly responsible for factual recall.
questions.explanation: Factual recall is linked to slow-wave sleep; REM supports creative problem solving.

questions.prompt: All sleep researchers agree on the value of laboratory experiments.
questions.explanation: Limitations are noted but no universal agreement is described.

questions.prompt: Sleeping after revision can improve test performance.
questions.explanation: Students who sleep after revision often perform better.

questions.prompt: Workplaces now completely ban overtime to protect sleep.
questions.explanation: Workplaces are increasingly aware of sleep loss, but no ban is mentioned.

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.
